title: Monorepo vs Multi-Repo — Source Control Strategy at FAANG Scale
subtitle: Why Google and Meta use monorepos while Amazon and Netflix use multi-repos — and how SAP's transport-based approach compares

sections.content:
## The Monorepo vs Multi-Repo Debate

The monorepo vs multi-repo decision is one of the most consequential infrastructure choices a growing engineering organization makes. It affects:

- **Developer velocity** — How fast can engineers find, modify, and ship code?
- **CI/CD complexity** — How fast are builds and tests?
- **Code quality** — How easy is it to enforce standards and share best practices?
- **Team autonomy** — How independent are teams?
- **Tooling investment** — What custom tooling is needed?

There is no universally correct answer. Google, Meta, and Microsoft use monorepos. Amazon, Netflix, and Uber use multi-repos. The right choice depends on your organization's scale, culture, and tooling investment.

For SAP architects: SAP's transport-based change management is a flavor of monorepo with an approval layer. Understanding FAANG approaches helps modernize SAP's development workflow.

cards.title: Monorepo — One Repository to Rule Them All
cards.body:
All code for all projects lives in a single repository. Every engineer has access to everything.

**Who uses it:** Google (Piper, 2B+ lines), Meta (6M+ commits/year), Microsoft (Windows, Office), Uber (before 2018).

**Advantages:**
- Atomic refactoring across all projects
- Shared tooling (one build system, one CI config)
- Code discovery (search across all projects)
- Standardized linting, formatting, security scanning
- Easy cross-team dependencies

**Disadvantages:**
- Giant codebase needs custom version control (Google uses Piper, not Git)
- CI builds can take hours even with Bazel/Turbo
- Access control is coarse (all-or-nothing read access)
- Not practical for open-source (can't give everyone access)

cards.title: Multi-Repo — Many Repositories, Many Teams
cards.body:
Each project, service, or library gets its own repository. Teams own their repos and control access.

**Who uses it:** Amazon, Netflix, Spotify, most startups and mid-size companies.

**Advantages:**
- Team autonomy (each team controls its repo, CI, release cycle)
- Smaller, faster builds (only the repo's code)
- Granular access control (per-repo permissions)
- Standard Git works (no custom VCS needed)
- Natural for open-source (one repo = one project)

**Disadvantages:**
- Cross-repo refactoring is painful (update 20 repos in sequence)
- Dependency hell (keep 50 package.json/lock files in sync)
- Inconsistent tooling (each repo has its own lint rules, CI config)
- Code discovery requires search across 100+ repos
- Duplicated boilerplate (auth, logging, error handling in every repo)

cards.title: The Hybrid "Monorail" Approach
cards.body:
Many companies use a hybrid: a monorepo for shared libraries and infrastructure, and separate repos for services.

**Google's approach:** Piper monorepo for first-party code, separate repos for open-source and external dependencies.

**Meta's approach:** Monorepo for all internal code, separate repos for open-source (React, PyTorch) mirroring from internal.

**Uber's evolution:** Started with monorepo, moved to multi-repo in 2018, then moved back to a "monorepo-ish" approach with Go monorepo + microservices.

**The pattern that works:**
- Monorepo for: shared libraries, configs, tooling, infrastructure-as-code
- Multi-repo for: services with independent release cycles, open-source projects
- Monorepo tooling: Bazel (Google), Buck (Meta), Turborepo (Vercel), Nx (Nrwl)

cards.title: SAP's Transport System — The SAP Monorepo
cards.body:
SAP's change management is conceptually a monorepo with an enforcement layer:

- All ABAP code lives in one system (one codebase per SAP system)
- Changes are packaged into transport requests
- Transports move through a landscape (DEV -> QAS -> PRD)
- Approval gates at each stage
- All developers see all code (within authorization scope)

**The Good:** Atomic changes, end-to-end traceability, standardized deployment.
**The Bad:** Merge conflicts (one person at a time per object), slow transport chains, no offline development, no Git history for individual changes.

**At FAANG scale, SAP would move to gCTS (Git-enabled Change and Transport System)** — treating ABAP repositories as Git monorepos with transport-based approval on top.

sections.title: FAANG Source Control Strategies Compared
rows: SAP's ABAP repository is conceptually similar (single codebase, shared dependencies)
Meta's monorepo with Mercurial maps to SAP's gCTS with Git
Git scaling (3,500+ engineers on same repo)
Windows monorepo maps to SAP's development with thousands of ABAP developers
Azure's multi-repo maps to SAP Cloud Platform with multiple BTP subaccounts
Amazon's service-per-repo maps to SAP's isolated systems per module
Netflix's microservice-per-repo maps to SAP's decoupled microservices
Uber's evolution maps to SAP's transition from ECC monolith to S/4HANA microservices

cards.title: The Build System Is the Bottleneck
cards.body:
At FAANG scale, the version control system matters less than the build system. Whether monorepo or multi-repo, the real constraint is: **how fast can a developer build and test their change?**

**Incremental builds (caching):**
- Bazel: Content-addressed cache. If inputs haven't changed, reuse cached output.
- Buck: Similar, with distributed caching across all developers.
- Nx/Turborepo: Task graph + caching for JS/TS monorepos.

**Distributed builds:**
- Bazel Remote Execution: Build on 1,000+ machines simultaneously.
- Microsoft's Cloud Build: Windows builds distributed across 10,000+ cores.

**Impact on monorepo adoption:** If you have fast, incremental builds, a monorepo is practical. If you don't, a monorepo turns into a 2-hour CI bottleneck.

**The Staff+ rule:** Before choosing monorepo or multi-repo, invest in your build system. Fast builds make either choice work. Slow builds make either choice painful.

cards.title: Dependency Management
cards.body:
Multi-repo companies face a fundamental challenge: **keeping dependencies in sync**.

**Library A v1.0 -> Service B**
- Library A releases v1.1 with a breaking change
- Service B must upgrade before it can use Library A's new features
- Service C, D, E also depend on Library A
- Coordinating upgrades across 10+ services is a project that takes weeks

**How monorepo solves this:** Atomic change. Update Library A to v1.1 AND update all consumers in the same commit. Every service is updated atomically.

**How multi-repo solves this:** Semantic versioning + automated dependency update tooling (Dependabot, Renovate). Services are updated asynchronously. Version conflicts are resolved at dependency resolution time.

**The key insight:** Monorepo optimizes for **change atomicity**. Multi-repo optimizes for **team autonomy**. Choose based on which is more important for your organization.

sections.title: Monorepo vs Multi-Repo Decision Framework
sections.code:
Decision framework for choosing source control strategy:

FACTOR 1: CODEBASE SIZE
  < 1M lines: Either works (standard Git can handle this)
  1M-100M lines: Monorepo with Bazel/Nx is practical
  > 100M lines: Monorepo needs custom VCS (Piper, custom Mercurial)
  
FACTOR 2: TEAM STRUCTURE
  ≤ 5 teams (all on same product): Monorepo (simpler)
  5-20 teams (shared platform): Monorepo for platform, repos for services
  > 20 teams (independent products): Multi-repo (autonomy outweighs atomicity)

FACTOR 3: BUILD SPEED
  < 5 min full build: Monorepo (no friction)
  5-30 min full build: Monorepo with incremental builds (Bazel/Nx)
  > 30 min full build: Multi-repo (isolated repos = faster per-repo builds)

FACTOR 4: DEPLOYMENT MODEL
  Single deployable unit (mobile app, monolith): Monorepo (atomic builds)
  Multiple services with independent deploys: Multi-repo (independent release cycles)
  Hybrid (monolith + services): Monorepo for shared code + service repos

FACTOR 5: OPEN SOURCE
  Internal-only code: Monorepo is fine
  Open-source projects: Multi-repo (one repo per OSS project)
  Both: Hybrid (monorepo for internal, sync to OSS repos)

QUICK GUIDE:
  Google/Meta style -> Monorepo
  Amazon/Netflix style -> Multi-repo
  Microsoft style -> Both (Windows monorepo, Azure multi-repo)
  SAP style -> Transport-based monorepo (can add Git layer via gCTS)

sections.content:
## SAP Bridge — From Transports to Git-Based Source Control

### Current State: SAP Transport System

- Changes are tracked via transport requests (CTS/CTO)
- Transports contain objects (programs, tables, function modules)
- One transport = one logical change
- Transports move through landscape: DEV -> QAS -> PRD
- Multiple transports can be grouped into transport of copies for consolidated moves

**Problems with transports:**
- No branching (all developers work on the same objects)
- No parallel development (first to lock an object wins)
- No Git history (transports are sequential, not branched)
- Merge conflicts are resolved by overwriting (no merge tool)
- Transport content is opaque (what really changed?)

### The Modern SAP Approach: gCTS (Git-Enabled CTS)

gCTS treats ABAP repositories as Git repositories. Each ABAP package maps to a Git repository. Developers:
1. Pull from Git (get latest code from main branch)
2. Make changes in ABAP Development Tools (Eclipse)
3. Commit locally (Git commit)
4. Push to remote Git (trigger CI/CD pipeline)
5. Transport request is created automatically from the Git diff

**How gCTS changes the game:**
| Aspect | Classic CTS | gCTS |
|---|---|---|
| Version control | Transport requests | Git (branch, commit, merge) |
| Parallel dev | Conflicts (serial via object locks) | Branch-based (Git merge) |
| History | Sequential transport list | Full Git history per package |
| CI/CD | Manual transport chain | Git push -> CI -> auto-import |
| Disaster recovery | Transport backup | Git repository (push to GitHub/GitLab) |

### The FAANG Perspective

For SAP architects: gCTS moves SAP from a legacy transport monorepo to a modern Git-based workflow. The principles are the same as FAANG monorepos: atomic changes, traceability, and CI/CD integration. The difference is SAP's enforcement layer (transports) on top of Git for compliance and audit requirements.

tabs.label: Recommendation
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Our company has 15 teams and a 2M-line codebase. Should we use monorepo or multi-repo?"

**Staff+ answer:** "I'd recommend a **hybrid monorepo** — one repository for shared platform code (libraries, APIs, configs), and separate repositories for each service.

**Rationale:**
- 15 teams is the inflection point where autonomy starts to matter
- 2M lines is manageable with standard Git + Bazel/Nx for incremental builds
- A shared platform monorepo gives you atomic refactoring of common dependencies
- Service repos give teams ownership over their deploy cycle

The key is the **build system**: invest in Bazel or Nx before the monorepo. If builds take < 5 minutes, the monorepo friction is acceptable. If builds take > 15 minutes, teams will revolt."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a specific recommendation (hybrid) with a concrete rationale (15-team inflection point, 2M-line threshold, build system dependency). Not a generic 'it depends.'

tabs.label: Migration
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "We have 200 repos and want to merge them into one. How?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Incremental, not Big Bang. Six phases:

**Phase 1: Audit** — Analyze all 200 repos. Identify the 50 most-depended-upon shared libraries. These go into the monorepo first.

**Phase 2: Monorepo foundation** — Create the monorepo with Bazel/Nx. Import the 50 shared libraries. Preserve their Git history using `git filter-repo` to keep author attribution.

**Phase 3: Library migration** — Each team migrates their consumers from the old repos to the monorepo packages. Use the migration to clean up APIs and standardize interfaces.

**Phase 4: Service migration** — Teams with services that depend heavily on monorepo libraries can optionally move their service code to the monorepo too. Services with independent release cycles stay in their own repos.

**Phase 5: Decommission** — Archive the old 50 repos. Configure Git to redirect old clone URLs to the monorepo.

**Phase 6: Iterate** — The monorepo grows organically as teams choose to move services in. No one is forced.

**Timeline:** 6-12 months for the full migration."

**Why this impresses:** You gave an incremental 6-phase plan with specific tooling (Bazel, git filter-repo), a timeline, and voluntary adoption (no forced migration). Realistic and implementable.

tabs.label: SAP Migration
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "How would you modernize SAP's transport-based development?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Move from classic CTS to gCTS (Git-enabled CTS) — this is the recommended migration path from SAP and FAANG-friendly.

**Phase 1: Setup gCTS** — Configure Git repositories per ABAP package. Set up GitHub/GitLab as the remote. Connect SAP system to remote Git.

**Phase 2: Developer workflow change** — Developers use ABAP Development Tools (Eclipse) with Git integration. Branch, commit, push instead of transport request creation. The transport request is an artifact of the Git push, not the primary change unit.

**Phase 3: CI/CD pipeline** — GitHub Actions/GitLab CI runs on every push: ABAP lint (ATC checks), unit tests, build validation. Only green builds are merged to main.

**Phase 4: Monorepo consolidation** — Move related ABAP packages into a monorepo structure (using SAP Package Hierarchy). One Git repo per SAP system landscape.

**Phase 5: GitFlow + transports** — Main branch maps to DEV. Release branches map to QAS. Tags trigger PRD imports. Transports become Git artifacts.

**The FAANG meta-point:** SAP doesn't need to abandon transports. It needs to add Git on top of transports — Git for developer workflow, transports for compliance audit."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a concrete 5-phase migration with specific SAP technologies (gCTS, ATC checks, SAP Package Hierarchy) and a meta-point about the relationship between Git and transports. Shows you understand both SAP's constraints and FAANG best practices.

tabs.label: Build System
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Why do companies like Google invest heavily in custom build systems like Bazel?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Because **the build system is the bottleneck for developer productivity** at scale.

Without Bazel, a monorepo of 2B+ lines would take days to build. With Bazel's incremental, distributed builds, Google developers see build times of < 30 seconds for most changes.

**Key Bazel innovations:**
1. **Content-addressed caching** — Every build artifact is keyed by the hash of its inputs. If inputs haven't changed, the cached output is used. No need to rebuild.
2. **Parallel dependency graph** — Bazel builds independent targets in parallel. A change to a deeply nested library doesn't block everything.
3. **Remote execution** — Build tasks run on a distributed cluster. A 10-minute build on a laptop takes 30 seconds on 1,000 cores.
4. **Reproducible builds** — Same inputs = same outputs. No 'works on my machine.'

**The meta-point:** Bazel enabled Google's monorepo, not the other way around. The decision order should be: build system first, monorepo second."

**Why this impresses:** You gave specific Bazel features (content-addressed cache, remote execution), a concrete benefit (30s instead of days), and the meta-point about decision ordering. Shows you understand the infrastructure behind the architecture debate.

cards.title: No Universal Right Answer
cards.body: Monorepo vs multi-repo depends on codebase size, team structure, build speed, deployment model, and open-source requirements. Understand the tradeoffs, don't pick a side blindly.

cards.title: Build System First, Repo Strategy Second
cards.body: A fast, incremental build system makes either choice work. Invest in Bazel, Nx, or Turborepo before deciding on repo strategy.

cards.title: Atomicity vs Autonomy
cards.body: Monorepo = atomic refactoring across all projects. Multi-repo = team autonomy and independent release cycles. Choose based on which tradeoff your organization values more.

cards.title: Hybrid Is Often Best
cards.body: Monorepo for shared platform code + separate repos for services with independent release cycles. This gives you the best of both worlds.

cards.title: SAP Is Already on a Monorepo Path
cards.body: gCTS (Git-enabled CTS) modernizes SAP's transport system to Git-based workflows. Migrating to gCTS is the FAANG-recommended path for SAP development.

cards.title: Dependency Management Is the Hidden Cost
cards.body: Multi-repo companies spend significant effort on dependency management (versioning, upgrading, coordinating). Monorepo eliminates this but requires a fast build system.
